mappings.rationaleAI: The British Empire's East India Company, Battle of Plassey (1757), and colonial governance in India comprehensively cover Anglo-French rivalry and British hegemony (A.3), extension of British power through the Company and Crown (B), and British imperial power including the raj and foreign policy (C).

The Mughal Empire's administrative system and decline provide context for the establishment of British trading posts alongside indigenous governance (A.3.a) and wars with the Marathas and Mysore (B.1.c).
Gandhi's satyagraha noncooperation policy, anti-British activity, and the broader Indian independence movement directly cover World War I's aftermath and the emergence of Gandhi (C.4).
Portuguese maritime exploration including Vasco da Gama's 1498 voyage to India directly covers Portuguese commercial relations with India from 1498 (A.1).
Akbar's reign (1556–1605) as Mughal emperor provides the context of indigenous governance during the period of establishment and growth of British settlements and trading posts (A.3.a).

European colonial domination through mercantilism and chartered companies (the East India Company) frames the entire section's treatment of European activity in India (A), extension of British power (B), and British imperial power (C).
The extension and maintenance of British power over India through military and economic coercion covers the extension of British power (B) and British imperial governance (C).

Bangladesh's territory as part of Bengal — the site of the British seizure of Calcutta (1757) and Clive's establishment of British control (A.3.c) — and securing of British supremacy in Bengal (B.1.a).
Pakistan's territory includes the Afghan and Sikh kingdoms annexed by the British in the 1840s and 1850s (B.1.f), and the North-West Frontier Province and Afghan conflicts (C.2).
India's contribution to the World War I effort and the post-war aftermath including the Amritsar massacre and Government of India Act (1919) directly cover C.4.

Tagore's critique of British imperialism, synthesis of Indian spiritual traditions with Enlightenment humanism, and educational reform at Visva-Bharati connect to the beginning of Indian nationalism (C.3) and the broader intellectual response to colonial rule (C.4).

Delhi as the capital of successive empires — the Delhi Sultanate and Mughal Empire — and its transformation during colonial rule connect to the political, legal, economic, social, and cultural effects of British influence (B.2).
The Industrial Revolution's impact on British economic policies in India connects to the political, legal, economic, social, and cultural effects of the first century of British influence (B.2).
The Silk Road's maritime routes and the decline that prompted European Age of Discovery provide context for Portuguese commercial relations with India from 1498 (A.1).
Spain's rivalry with Portugal, including the Treaty of Tordesillas, provides context for Portuguese commercial relations and European competition in India (A.1).
